title: Geodesics & Hyperbolicity of Taxicab Half-Plane Space
text: With Kevin Lin, Alina Potapyev, and faculty advisor Dr. Dylan Helliwell, Department of Mathematics, Seattle University. Funded by the College of Science and Engineering Summer Research program. Presented at the 2025 Seattle University STEM Research Summit and the 2026 PNW MAA conference.

title: Geodesics & Hyperbolicity of Taxicab Half-Plane Space
text:
Poster


Download the poster (PDF)

title: Geodesics & Hyperbolicity of Taxicab Half-Plane Space
text:
What the research is about
We built a new geometric space by blending the half-plane model of the hyperbolic plane with taxicab (\(L^1\)) geometry, then asked whether the result is still “hyperbolic” in the rigorous sense Gromov defined for general metric spaces — a property that underlies a lot of modern geometric group theory.
For \(m \in [0,1]\), the space is \(\left(\mathbb{H}_m^2, \lVert\cdot\rVert_m\right)\), where
\[\mathbb{H}_m^2 = \{x = (x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : x_2 > m x_1\}, \qquad \lVert v \rVert_{m,x} = \frac{|v_1| + |v_2|}{x_2 - m x_1}.\]
Instead of the usual Euclidean norm on each tangent space (as in the standard hyperbolic half-plane), we put a taxicab norm there, scaled by distance to a boundary line at infinity that tilts as \(m\) varies from \(0\) (horizontal boundary) toward \(1\).
Geodesics. By minimizing arc length \(L(\gamma) = \int_0^1 \lVert \gamma'(t)\rVert_m\, dt\) over curves connecting two points, we showed the shortest paths are concatenations of a coordinate-horizontal and a coordinate-vertical segment — an axis-aligned “corner” path, very much in the spirit of taxicab geometry, but now living on a curved boundary. This directly produces a closed-form distance function on \(\mathbb{H}_m^2\).
Hyperbolicity. With a distance function in hand, we tested Gromov’s \(\delta\)-hyperbolicity condition: for all points \(x,y,z,w\),
\[G(x,y;w) \ge \min\{G(x,z;w),\, G(y,z;w)\} - \delta, \qquad G(x,y;z) = \tfrac12\big(d(x,z)+d(y,z)\big) - d(x,y).\]
Through case analysis on the geometry of how points sit relative to the boundary at infinity, we derived an explicit bound
\[f(x,y,z,w) \le \tfrac12 \ln\!\left(\frac{\Omega(x,y)\,\Omega(z,w)}{\max\{\Omega(x,z)\Omega(y,w),\ \Omega(x,w)\Omega(y,z)\}}\right) + \tfrac{3}{2m}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+m}{1-m}\right),\]
showing \(\mathbb{H}_m^2\) is \(\delta\)-hyperbolic for a \(\delta\) depending only on \(m\).
Open questions. The \(m=1\) (fully slanted) case, extensions to higher dimensions with horizontal or slanted hyperplanes at infinity, and behavior under quotient spaces are all open — see the poster’s Future Work panel.

title: Geodesics & Hyperbolicity of Taxicab Half-Plane Space
AMR Load Balancing: LPT vs. Hilbert Curve Scheduling
text: Full source and companion notebooks: github.com/diffeqsolver/amr-load-balancing-simulation.

title: AMR Load Balancing: LPT vs. Hilbert Curve Scheduling
text:
The problem
In parallel scientific computing, solving PDEs on high-resolution grids is expensive. Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) helps by dynamically allocating high-resolution blocks only where the physics is steep (shock fronts, combustion fronts) and keeping the rest of the domain coarse.
That creates a new problem: once the grid is refined, the resulting blocks — of varying size and cost — must be distributed across \(P\) processors. Two costs compete:

Computational load imbalance — the slowest processor sets the pace for the whole step.
Communication overhead — neighboring blocks assigned to different processors must exchange boundary (guard-cell) data over the network, which is slow.

For a communication-cost scaling factor \(\beta\), the makespan of a step is:
\[\text{Makespan} = \max_{p} \left( T_{\text{comp}}(p) + T_{\text{comm}}(p) \right)\]
This notebook-turned-article builds a 2D AMR grid around a moving shock wave, then compares two classic partitioning heuristics on that exact makespan.

title: AMR Load Balancing: LPT vs. Hilbert Curve Scheduling
AMR Load Balancing: LPT vs. Hilbert Curve Scheduling
text:
Two scheduling heuristics
LPT (Largest Processing Time) sorts blocks by workload, descending, and greedily assigns each to the currently least-loaded processor. It’s spatially blind — great load balance, no regard for locality.
Hilbert space-filling curve (SFC) maps each block’s 2D center onto a 1D Hilbert index, sorts by that index, and slices the resulting line into \(P\) contiguous chunks. Because the Hilbert curve preserves 2D locality, this keeps neighboring blocks together — at some cost to perfect load balance.


Code
def xy2d(n, x, y):
    d = 0
    s = n // 2
    while s > 0:
        rx = (x & s) > 0
        ry = (y & s) > 0
        d += s * s * ((3 * rx) ^ ry)
        if ry == 0:
            if rx == 1:
                x, y = n - 1 - x, n - 1 - y
            x, y = y, x
        s //= 2
    return d


def lpt_schedule(env, num_processors):
    num_blocks = len(env.blocks)
    assignment = np.zeros(num_blocks, dtype=int)
    block_workloads = sorted(
        [(i, block["workload"]) for i, block in enumerate(env.blocks)], key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True
    )
    processor_loads = np.zeros(num_processors)
    for idx, workload in block_workloads:
        min_proc = np.argmin(processor_loads)
        assignment[idx] = min_proc
        processor_loads[min_proc] += workload
    return assignment


def hilbert_schedule(env, num_processors):
    num_blocks = len(env.blocks)
    if num_blocks == 0:
        return np.array([], dtype=int)
    n_hilbert = 2 ** int(np.ceil(np.log2(env.domain_size)))
    block_hilbert = []
    for i, b in enumerate(env.blocks):
        cx, cy = b["x"] + b["size"] / 2.0, b["y"] + b["size"] / 2.0
        ix = int(np.clip(cx, 0, n_hilbert - 1))
        iy = int(np.clip(cy, 0, n_hilbert - 1))
        block_hilbert.append((i, xy2d(n_hilbert, ix, iy), b["workload"]))
    block_hilbert.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
    sorted_indices = [x[0] for x in block_hilbert]
    sorted_workloads = [x[2] for x in block_hilbert]
    prefix_sums = np.cumsum(sorted_workloads)
    total_load = prefix_sums[-1]
    targets = [k * (total_load / num_processors) for k in range(1, num_processors)]
    split_indices = [np.argmin(np.abs(prefix_sums - t)) for t in targets]
    sorted_assignment = np.zeros(num_blocks, dtype=int)
    p = 0
    for i in range(num_blocks):
        sorted_assignment[i] = p
        if p < len(split_indices) and i == split_indices[p]:
            p += 1
    assignment = np.zeros(num_blocks, dtype=int)
    for sorted_idx, orig_idx in enumerate(sorted_indices):
        assignment[orig_idx] = sorted_assignment[sorted_idx]
    return assignment

title: AMR Load Balancing: LPT vs. Hilbert Curve Scheduling
text:
Generating a refined grid
A diagonal shock wave at \(x\cos(\pi/4) + y\sin(\pi/4) = 11.3\) drives the refinement: blocks near the front split down to level 2, and blocks intersecting the shock get up to 5x the workload of a quiet-region block.


Code
def plot_grid(env, assignment=None, num_processors=4, title="AMR Grid Partitioning",
              shock_pos=8.0, shock_angle=np.pi / 4, shock_width=2.5, ax=None):
    if ax is None:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
    ax.set_xlim(0, env.domain_size)
    ax.set_ylim(0, env.domain_size)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")

    if assignment is not None:
        cmap = plt.colormaps.get_cmap("tab10")
        colors = [cmap(i % 10) for i in range(num_processors)]
    else:
        level_colors = {0: "#e0f3f8", 1: "#9ecae1", 2: "#3182bd"}

    for i, block in enumerate(env.blocks):
        x, y, size = block["x"], block["y"], block["size"]
        facecolor = colors[assignment[i]] if assignment is not None else level_colors.get(block["level"], "#ffffff")
        rect = patches.Rectangle((x, y), size, size, linewidth=1.0, edgecolor="#333333", facecolor=facecolor, alpha=0.85)
        ax.add_patch(rect)

    cos_a, sin_a = np.cos(shock_angle), np.sin(shock_angle)
    x_vals = np.linspace(0, env.domain_size, 100)
    y_vals = (shock_pos - x_vals * cos_a) / sin_a
    valid = (y_vals >= 0) & (y_vals <= env.domain_size)
    ax.plot(x_vals[valid], y_vals[valid], color="#e31a1c", linestyle="--", linewidth=2, label="Shock front")

    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11, weight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("X")
    ax.set_ylabel("Y")
    ax.grid(True, linestyle=":", alpha=0.4)
    if assignment is not None:
        handles = [patches.Patch(facecolor=colors[p], edgecolor="#333333", label=f"Proc {p}") for p in range(num_processors)]
    else:
        handles = [patches.Patch(facecolor=level_colors[lvl], edgecolor="#333333", label=f"Level {lvl}") for lvl in [0, 1, 2]]
    ax.legend(handles=handles, loc="upper right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
    return ax


domain_size, base_block_size, beta = 16.0, 4.0, 0.3
shock_pos, shock_angle = 11.3, np.pi / 4

env = AMREnvironment(domain_size, base_block_size, beta)
env.generate_grid(shock_pos=shock_pos, shock_angle=shock_angle, shock_width=2.2, shock_intensity=5.0)

print(f"Generated {len(env.blocks)} leaf blocks, total workload {sum(b['workload'] for b in env.blocks):.2f}")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
plot_grid(env, title="AMR refinement levels near the shock front", shock_pos=shock_pos, shock_angle=shock_angle, shock_width=2.2, ax=ax)
plt.show()


Generated 52 leaf blocks, total workload 170.46

title: AMR Load Balancing: LPT vs. Hilbert Curve Scheduling
section: LPT vs. Hilbert, side by side
text:
LPT vs. Hilbert, side by side


Code
num_processors = 4
lpt_assign = lpt_schedule(env, num_processors)
hilbert_assign = hilbert_schedule(env, num_processors)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 6))
plot_grid(env, assignment=lpt_assign, num_processors=num_processors,
          title="LPT — balanced load, fragmented domains", shock_pos=shock_pos, shock_angle=shock_angle, shock_width=2.2, ax=axes[0])
plot_grid(env, assignment=hilbert_assign, num_processors=num_processors,
          title="Hilbert SFC — contiguous bands", shock_pos=shock_pos, shock_angle=shock_angle, shock_width=2.2, ax=axes[1])
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









LPT scatters same-colored blocks across the whole domain — perfectly balanced compute, but neighboring blocks constantly land on different processors, driving up communication. Hilbert keeps each processor’s blocks in a contiguous spatial band, cutting communication at the cost of a slightly less even load.

title: AMR Load Balancing: LPT vs. Hilbert Curve Scheduling
text:
Makespan comparison


Code
import pandas as pd

results = {
    "LPT (greedy bin-pack)": env.evaluate_assignment(lpt_assign, num_processors),
    "Hilbert curve SFC": env.evaluate_assignment(hilbert_assign, num_processors),
}
pd.DataFrame(results).T[["makespan", "comp_makespan", "total_comm", "load_imbalance"]].round(3)

title: AMR Load Balancing: LPT vs. Hilbert Curve Scheduling
text:
Takeaways

LPT wins on raw load balance (imbalance often under 1%) but pays for it in communication cost — its partitions are spatially fragmented.
Hilbert SFC accepts a slightly higher load imbalance in exchange for dramatically lower communication cost, since it preserves spatial locality.
Which one wins on makespan depends on \(\beta\) — the relative cost of communication vs. computation. On a high-latency interconnect, Hilbert’s locality advantage outweighs its imbalance; on a fast one, LPT’s tighter balance can win outright.
The open problem: a scheduler that gets LPT’s load-splitting power and Hilbert’s spatial awareness, cheaply enough to run every refinement step. That combination is still an active area in HPC scheduling research.

title: Gaussian Process Metamodeling for Financial Valuation
text:
Note



This page is a working outline — full write-up and code walkthrough coming later. The underlying dataset and production pipeline are not public, so this project won’t have an executable demo like the Heston model; this outline covers the methodology and findings.

title: Gaussian Process Metamodeling for Financial Valuation
text:
Problem

Predict fair-market value across a 190,000-contract variable-annuity portfolio without running the full, expensive actuarial valuation on every contract.
Goal: a fast, accurate metamodel that approximates the expensive valuation function well enough to replace it in scenario analysis.

title: Gaussian Process Metamodeling for Financial Valuation
text:
Data pipeline

Latin Hypercube scenario reduction to cut down the number of economic scenarios needed for training while preserving coverage of the input space.
One-hot encoding of categorical contract features.
Feature scaling pipeline ahead of GP training.

title: Gaussian Process Metamodeling for Financial Valuation
text:
Modeling

Gaussian Process regression via GPyTorch.
Benchmarked 6 GP configurations:

Kernels: RBF, Matérn-5/2, Exponential
Mean priors: constant, linear

Baselines: k-nearest neighbors, linear regression.

title: Gaussian Process Metamodeling for Financial Valuation
text:
Evaluation

Metrics: R², RMSE, BIC.
GP models outperformed both baselines on accuracy across the board.
ARD (Automatic Relevance Determination) lengthscales analyzed post-hoc to rank predictor significance — i.e., which contract/scenario features the model relies on most.

title: Gaussian Process Metamodeling for Financial Valuation
text:
Stack
Python · GPyTorch · scikit-learn · Pandas

title: Stochastic Volatility & Option Pricing — Heston Model
text:
This project implements a hybrid C++/Python option pricing framework under the Heston stochastic volatility model, comparing a multi-threaded Monte Carlo path simulator against a closed-form semi-analytic Fourier transform pricer. The full build-up — from first-principles probability, to Brownian motion, to SDEs, to the Heston model itself — along with all source code, lives in the GitHub repo.
The asset price and its instantaneous variance evolve as a pair of correlated SDEs:
\[dS_t = \mu S_t\, dt + \sqrt{v_t}\, S_t\, dW_t^{(1)}\] \[dv_t = \kappa(\theta - v_t)\, dt + \xi \sqrt{v_t}\, dW_t^{(2)}\]
with \(dW_t^{(1)} dW_t^{(2)} = \rho\, dt\).

title: Stochastic Volatility & Option Pricing — Heston Model
text:
This project implements a hybrid C++/Python option pricing framework under the Heston stochastic volatility model, comparing a multi-threaded Monte Carlo path simulator against a closed-form semi-analytic Fourier transform pricer. The full build-up — from first-principles probability, to Brownian motion, to SDEs, to the Heston model itself — along with all source code, lives in the GitHub repo.
The asset price and its instantaneous variance evolve as a pair of correlated SDEs:
\[dS_t = \mu S_t\, dt + \sqrt{v_t}\, S_t\, dW_t^{(1)}\] \[dv_t = \kappa(\theta - v_t)\, dt + \xi \sqrt{v_t}\, dW_t^{(2)}\]
with \(dW_t^{(1)} dW_t^{(2)} = \rho\, dt\).

title: Stochastic Volatility & Option Pricing — Heston Model
text:
What I built

A semi-analytic Fourier pricer using the Gatheral/Albrecher stable formulation of the Heston characteristic function, integrated via scipy.integrate.quad.
A multi-threaded Monte Carlo engine written in C++ (Log-Euler scheme for the price process, full-truncation for variance) and exposed to Python via pybind11.
Simulated up to 100K paths to evaluate Monte Carlo convergence against the semi-analytic baseline with 95% confidence intervals.
A custom Matplotlib plotting suite for dual-axis overlays and side-by-side benchmark visualizations.

title: Stochastic Volatility & Option Pricing — Heston Model
text:
Results
A simulated Heston path, showing the asset price responding to its own stochastic, mean-reverting variance process:



Simulated asset price with its overlaid instantaneous variance path.


Monte Carlo price estimates converge tightly to the semi-analytic solution as path count grows, and the C++ engine delivers a consistent speedup over the pure-Python Monte Carlo implementation:



Monte Carlo convergence to the semi-analytic price (left) and C++ vs. Python runtime across path counts (right).


The multi-threaded C++ implementation achieves roughly a 3–5x speedup over pure Python at large path counts, while both converge to the same semi-analytic option price of 10.39.

title: Stochastic Volatility & Option Pricing — Heston Model
text:
Stack
Python · C++ (pybind11, C++11 threads) · NumPy · SciPy · Matplotlib

title: Luke Lawrence
text:
Hi, I’m Luke — a Math major (Computer Science minor) at Seattle University, expected June 2027, working at the intersection of stochastic calculus, machine learning, and numerical computing.
My recent work spans stochastic volatility modeling and option pricing, Gaussian process metamodeling for financial valuation, and undergraduate research in non-Euclidean geometry (recipient of the Jane E. Mills Award for Undergraduate Research in Mathematics). I’ve also shipped full-stack, LLM-backed product features as a software engineering intern.

Projects — quant finance, ML, and scientific computing builds
Research — write-ups and explorations

Reach me at lukalawrence24@gmail.com, on GitHub, or on LinkedIn.
